critic_raw:
You are reviewing an ML project. A genomics startup trained a deep sequence model to predict whether missense variants in an inherited cardiomyopathy panel are pathogenic...

CRITIQUE:

This genomics evaluation has three methodological problems that inflate the reported AUROC gap (0.93 vs. 0.84).

1. [PANEL-SPECIFIC-VS-GENERIC-THRESHOLDING] PANEL-SPECIFIC vs. GENERIC THRESHOLDING: The startup's model was calibrated and thresholded specifically on missense variants from cardiomyopathy genes. PolyPhen-2 is used with its generic published threshold, which was not calibrated for cardiomyopathy-specific variants. In any threshold-dependent performance metric, a model evaluated at its optimized domain-specific threshold will outperform one evaluated at a generic threshold designed for the full variant space. This is not evidence of model quality; it is evidence of threshold calibration.

2. [HOTSPOT-GENE-COMPOSITION-ADVANTAGE] HOTSPOT GENE COMPOSITION ADVANTAGE: Approximately 40% of the evaluation set comes from two sarcomere genes with unusually well-characterized pathogenic hotspots. The startup's deep sequence model, trained on this gene panel with protein-language-model embeddings, likely benefits from learning the hotspot patterns in these two genes. PolyPhen-2's generic scoring was not designed for this distribution. If 40% of the evaluation set is dominated by variants in two hotspot-rich genes, performance on that subset could be driving the bulk of the AUROC advantage, and that advantage may not generalize to the broader cardiomyopathy panel.

3. [LIMITED-BASELINE-SET-FOR-MODERN-CLAIM] LIMITED BASELINE SET FOR MODERN CLAIM: The paper compares against PolyPhen-2, a legacy predictor, and frames the result as showing 'modern foundation-model representations outperform classical variant-effect predictors.' Modern variant pathogenicity tools (AlphaMissense, EVE, ESM-1v, CADD) are not included. A claim about foundation-model representations requires at least one modern deep-learning baseline trained on similar data types. Comparing against only a circa-2002 tool and claiming foundation-model superiority is an inadequate evaluation.
defender_raw:
You are reviewing an ML project. A genomics startup trained a deep sequence model to predict whether missense variants in an inherited cardiomyopathy panel are pathogenic...

DEFENSE:

The evaluation is appropriate for the intended clinical use case.

1. DOMAIN-SPECIFIC CALIBRATION IS APPROPRIATE: The model is intended as a gene-panel-specific decision support tool. Calibrating the threshold on cardiomyopathy variants is correct for the intended deployment context. Clinicians in inherited cardiac care use PolyPhen-2 off the shelf — the comparison reflects reality.

2. HOTSPOT GENES REPRESENT REAL CLINICAL BURDEN: The two sarcomere genes with well-characterized pathogenic hotspots are clinically important and commonly encountered in cardiomyopathy panels. Performing well on these genes is clinically relevant, not a methodological artifact.

3. POLYPHEN-2 IS THE STANDARD COMPARATOR: PolyPhen-2 is widely used in tertiary clinical analysis pipelines. Demonstrating improvement over what clinicians currently use is the relevant comparison for a clinical decision support tool.

4. LABEL CURATION IS APPROPRIATE: Excluding uncertain variants and retaining only binary ClinVar labels after curator review is methodologically sound for a pathogenicity classification benchmark.
adjudication_raw: The defender's argument is that PolyPhen-2 is what clinicians use and domain-specific calibration is appropriate. These are deployment arguments, not scientific validity arguments. The three issues are analytically distinct: (1) threshold calibration asymmetry means any model at its optimized threshold will outperform one at a generic threshold, independent of intrinsic quality; (2) hotspot gene composition means a disproportionate share of easy-to-classify variants may be driving the AUROC gap; (3) comparing against only a circa-2002 tool for a claim about 'modern foundation-model representations' is an inadequate standard of evidence. The defender's points about clinical relevance do not address whether the AUROC gap is an accurate measure of model quality. All three critiques are valid. Critique wins.
